seoTitle: Aurasemi Products | Clock, Power, RF & Sensor ICs | LiTong Distributor
seoDescription: Explore Aurasemi product portfolio including clock generators, power management ICs, RF chips, and MEMS sensors. Authorized distributor with technical support and competitive pricing.

faqs.question: What are the main product categories offered by Aurasemi?
faqs.answer: Aurasemi offers four main product categories: (1) Clock Chips - high-precision clock generators, jitter attenuators, clock buffers, PCIe clock drivers, and real-time clocks for networking and communication systems; (2) Power Management ICs - VRM controllers, DC-DC converters, linear regulators, and power modules for servers and automotive applications; (3) RF Chips - power amplifiers, analog front-end modules, RF switches, and pre-amplifiers for wireless communication; (4) MEMS Sensors - accelerometers, gyroscopes, position sensors, and IoT sensors for automotive and industrial applications. Each category includes multiple series optimized for specific performance requirements and operating environments.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review each product category to find the Aurasemi component matching your application requirements.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Aurasemi clock chip for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Aurasemi clock chip depends on your application requirements: (1) For high-speed networking (10G/25G/100G Ethernet) - choose AU54xx series jitter attenuators with <100fs RMS jitter; (2) For PCIe Gen3/Gen4 applications - choose AU50xx series PCIe clock generators; (3) For general-purpose clock distribution - choose AU48xx series clock buffers; (4) For automotive applications - choose AEC-Q100 qualified AU55xx series. Key parameters to consider include output frequency range, jitter performance, number of outputs, supply voltage, and temperature range. Contact LiTong FAE for detailed selection guidance and reference designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match the clock chip to your jitter requirements, output configuration, and application environment.

faqs.question: What are the key features of Aurasemi power management ICs?
faqs.answer: Aurasemi power management ICs feature: (1) High efficiency - up to 96% efficiency with advanced control algorithms; (2) Wide input voltage range - supporting 2.5V to 60V input for various applications; (3) Fast transient response - optimized for processor and FPGA power rails; (4) Comprehensive protection - including OVP, UVP, OCP, and thermal shutdown; (5) Small footprint - available in compact QFN and WLCSP packages; (6) Automotive qualification - AEC-Q100 Grade 1 options available. The AU80xx series VRM controllers are specifically designed for server and networking applications with PMBus support for digital power management.
faqs.decisionGuide: Consider Aurasemi power management for high-efficiency, compact solutions with comprehensive protection features.

faqs.question: How do Aurasemi MEMS sensors compare to competitors?
faqs.answer: Aurasemi MEMS sensors offer competitive advantages: (1) High precision - accelerometers with ±2g to ±16g range and 0.5% linearity; (2) Low power consumption - <10μA in sleep mode for battery-powered applications; (3) Wide temperature range - -40°C to +125°C for industrial and automotive use; (4) Integrated features - built-in temperature compensation and self-test capabilities; (5) Cost-effective pricing - 20-30% lower than established competitors while maintaining quality; (6) Local support - rapid technical support from Aurasemi and LiTong engineering teams. The AU90xx series accelerometers and AU91xx series gyroscopes are pin-compatible with popular competitors for easy substitution.
faqs.decisionGuide: Aurasemi sensors offer excellent price-performance ratio with local technical support.

faqs.question: What is the quality and reliability of Aurasemi products?
faqs.answer: Aurasemi maintains high quality and reliability standards: (1) Design quality - rigorous design verification and simulation processes; (2) Manufacturing - partnership with Tier 1 foundries including TSMC and SMIC; (3) Testing - 100% electrical testing and burn-in for industrial/automotive grades; (4) Qualification - JEDEC standard qualification with HTOL, TCT, and ESD testing; (5) Automotive certification - AEC-Q100 Grade 1 and Grade 2 qualified products available; (6) Traceability - full lot traceability for quality tracking; (7) Reliability data - published MTBF and FIT rates for all product families. Aurasemi's quality management system is ISO 9001 certified.
faqs.decisionGuide: Aurasemi products meet industry-standard quality and reliability requirements for commercial, industrial, and automotive applications.

categories.name: Clock Chips
categories.description: Aurasemi clock chips provide high-precision timing solutions for networking, data centers, and communication systems. The portfolio includes clock generators with ultra-low jitter (<100fs RMS), jitter attenuators for synchronizing with reference clocks, clock buffers for distributing signals to multiple loads, and PCIe clock drivers compliant with Gen3/Gen4 specifications. These products support output frequencies from 1MHz to 2.1GHz with various output types including LVDS, LVPECL, HCSL, and CMOS. Aurasemi clock solutions are designed for 5G base stations, 100G/400G Ethernet switches, high-speed ADC/DAC systems, and FPGA/ASIC clocking applications.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Aurasemi Clock Chips
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide to selecting the right clock generator, jitter attenuator, or clock buffer for your application

faqs.question: What is the difference between clock generators and jitter attenuators?
faqs.answer: Clock generators (synthesizers) create clock signals from a reference crystal or oscillator, generating multiple output frequencies using PLL technology. They are ideal when you need to generate various clock frequencies from a single crystal reference. Jitter attenuators (cleaners) accept an existing clock input with high jitter and output a cleaned, low-jitter version using high-performance PLLs with narrow loop bandwidth. They are essential when receiving clock signals from long traces or noisy environments. Aurasemi offers both types: AU50xx series clock generators for frequency synthesis and AU54xx series jitter attenuators for jitter cleaning applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use clock generators for frequency synthesis; use jitter attenuators for cleaning noisy clock signals.

faqs.question: How do I interpret jitter specifications in Aurasemi clock chips?
faqs.answer: Jitter specifications in Aurasemi clock chips include: (1) RMS jitter - the root-mean-square of phase noise integrated over a frequency range (typically 12kHz to 20MHz), measured in femtoseconds (fs). Lower values indicate cleaner clocks. Aurasemi's AU5425 achieves <100fs RMS jitter; (2) Peak-to-peak jitter - the maximum deviation over time, important for synchronous systems; (3) Phase noise - measured in dBc/Hz at specific offsets from the carrier, showing noise distribution across frequencies. For high-speed SerDes (10G+), RMS jitter <500fs is typically required. For precision ADC/DAC applications, <100fs may be needed. Check your system's jitter tolerance specifications when selecting.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select clock chips based on your system's jitter requirements - <100fs for high-performance, <500fs for general high-speed.

faqs.question: What output types are available in Aurasemi clock chips?
faqs.answer: Aurasemi clock chips support multiple output types: (1) LVDS (Low-Voltage Differential Signaling) - 3.5mA current-mode differential outputs, low power, good for general-purpose high-speed clocking; (2) LVPECL (Low-Voltage Positive Emitter-Coupled Logic) - high-speed differential outputs with excellent jitter performance, ideal for 10G+ networking; (3) HCSL (High-Speed Current Steering Logic) - low-power differential outputs specifically designed for PCIe clocking; (4) CMOS/LVTTL - single-ended outputs for lower frequency or cost-sensitive applications; (5) CML (Current Mode Logic) - used for very high-speed SerDes clocking. Many Aurasemi devices support multiple output types through pin configuration or register settings for design flexibility.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose LVPECL/HCSL for high-speed differential, LVDS for general-purpose, CMOS for single-ended applications.

faqs.question: How do I power and decouple Aurasemi clock chips?
faqs.answer: Proper power supply design is critical for clock performance: (1) Use clean, low-noise power supplies - Aurasemi clock chips typically require 1.8V, 2.5V, or 3.3V with <50mV ripple; (2) Implement proper decoupling - place 0.1μF and 1μF ceramic capacitors close to each power pin, with the smaller capacitor closest to the pin; (3) Use separate power planes - isolate analog (PLL) and digital power supplies if possible; (4) Add ferrite beads - series ferrite beads on power lines can help isolate noise; (5) Grounding - use solid ground planes and minimize ground loops; (6) For multi-rail devices, follow the recommended power-up sequence in the datasheet. Poor power supply design can degrade jitter performance by 2-3x.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use clean power with proper decoupling to achieve specified jitter performance.

faqs.question: What is the recommended PCB layout for Aurasemi clock chips?
faqs.answer: Optimal PCB layout for Aurasemi clock chips: (1) Keep crystal/oscillator traces short - minimize length between crystal and chip (<10mm ideal); (2) Differential routing - route differential clock pairs with matched length and controlled impedance (100Ω for LVDS/LVPECL); (3) Avoid vias - minimize vias on critical clock traces; (4) Ground planes - use continuous ground planes under clock circuits; (5) Isolation - keep clock traces away from switching power supplies and high-speed digital signals; (6) Termination - place termination resistors close to load devices; (7) Thermal design - ensure adequate copper area for thermal dissipation. Following these guidelines helps achieve the specified jitter performance and signal integrity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow differential routing best practices and minimize trace lengths for optimal clock performance.

products.name: Ultra-Low Jitter Clock Generator
products.shortDescription: High-performance clock generator with 4 differential outputs, <100fs RMS jitter, supporting frequencies up to 2.1GHz for 5G and data center applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The AU5425 is a high-performance clock generator designed for demanding networking and communication applications. It features an integrated high-performance PLL that generates ultra-low jitter clock outputs from a crystal or reference clock input.

With RMS jitter performance below 100 femtoseconds (12kHz to 20MHz integration), the AU5425 meets the stringent requirements of 100G/400G Ethernet, high-speed ADC/DAC systems, and FPGA clocking applications. The device provides four differential outputs configurable as LVDS, LVPECL, or HCSL.
The AU5425 supports output frequencies from 1MHz to 2.1GHz through an I2C/SPI programmable interface. It operates from a 1.8V, 2.5V, or 3.3V supply and is available in a compact 5x5mm QFN package.
applications: 5G base station and small cell equipment

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Timing Solutions
faeReview.content: The AU5425 is my go-to recommendation for customers needing ultra-low jitter clocking. In my experience supporting 5G and data center designs, this device consistently delivers the <100fs jitter performance needed for 100G+ SerDes applications. I particularly appreciate the flexible output configuration - being able to switch between LVDS, LVPECL, and HCSL through register settings saves board space and simplifies BOM management. The I2C interface makes it easy to fine-tune frequencies during bring-up. One tip: pay close attention to power supply noise - use a dedicated LDO for the PLL supply to achieve the best jitter performance. I've seen customers achieve 80-90fs typical jitter with clean power design.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low jitter clock generator ideal for 100G+ networking and 5G applications

alternativeParts.reason: More outputs for complex clock trees, higher cost
Dual PLL architecture for jitter cleaning, more complex
alternativeParts.useCase: Systems requiring jitter attenuation + clock generation

companionParts.description: 1:4 LVDS clock buffer for distributing AU5425 outputs
PCIe Gen4 clock generator for companion PCIe interfaces
Low-noise LDO for clean PLL power supply

faqs.question: What is the typical jitter performance of AU5425 in real applications?
faqs.answer: In typical applications with proper PCB layout and clean power supply, the AU5425 achieves 80-90fs RMS jitter (12kHz to 20MHz integration). The datasheet specifies <100fs as a maximum guarantee, but most devices perform significantly better. Factors affecting jitter include: power supply noise (use dedicated LDO), PCB layout (follow differential routing guidelines), temperature (performance is stable across -40°C to +85°C), and output loading (maintain proper termination). For the lowest jitter, use the LVPECL output mode and ensure the crystal reference has low phase noise (<-150dBc/Hz at 1kHz offset).
faqs.decisionGuide: Expect 80-90fs typical jitter with good design practices; <100fs guaranteed.

faqs.question: How do I program the output frequency of AU5425?
faqs.answer: The AU5425 supports frequency programming through I2C or SPI interface: (1) Calculate PLL parameters using Aurasemi's Clock Architect software or manual calculation; (2) Write divider values to registers 0x00-0x0F for the four output channels; (3) Configure output type (LVDS/LVPECL/HCSL) in register 0x10; (4) Enable outputs by writing to register 0x11; (5) Lock the PLL by setting register 0x12. The device supports integer and fractional PLL modes. For fractional mode, set the fractional divider in registers 0x20-0x23. The default power-on frequency is determined by external strapping pins, allowing operation without programming if the default meets your requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use I2C/SPI interface to program output frequencies; default strapping available for common frequencies.

faqs.question: What crystal specifications are recommended for AU5425?
faqs.answer: Recommended crystal specifications for AU5425: (1) Frequency - 25MHz or 50MHz are most common, but 10MHz to 50MHz range is supported; (2) Load capacitance - 8pF to 20pF, match to your PCB design; (3) ESR - <50Ω for reliable startup; (4) Drive level - 100μW typical, 200μW maximum; (5) Frequency stability - ±20ppm for general applications, ±10ppm for high-performance; (6) Aging - ±3ppm/year maximum; (7) Package - HC-49/S or compact 3.2x2.5mm SMD. For best jitter performance, select a crystal with low phase noise. Aurasemi recommends crystals from Epson, NDK, or TXC. The crystal should be placed close to the AU5425 (<10mm trace length) with proper load capacitors.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 25MHz or 50MHz crystal with <50Ω ESR and ±20ppm stability for best performance.

faqs.question: Can AU5425 be used for PCIe clocking applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, the AU5425 can be used for PCIe clocking with proper configuration: (1) Set output type to HCSL for PCIe compliance; (2) Configure 100MHz output frequency (PCIe Gen1/Gen2/Gen3) or 25MHz reference; (3) Enable spread spectrum clocking (SSC) by setting register 0x15 to 0x03 for -0.5% downspread; (4) Ensure <3ps cycle-to-cycle jitter for PCIe Gen3 compliance; (5) Use proper HCSL termination (50Ω to ground on each output). However, for dedicated PCIe applications, Aurasemi recommends the AU5061 PCIe clock generator which includes built-in PCIe compliance features, reference clock inputs, and simpler configuration. The AU5425 is better suited as a general-purpose clock generator that can also support PCIe among other protocols.
faqs.decisionGuide: AU5425 can support PCIe; consider AU5061 for dedicated PCIe applications with built-in compliance features.

faqs.question: What is the power consumption of AU5425?
faqs.answer: AU5425 power consumption varies by configuration: (1) Core power - 45mA typical at 3.3V with all outputs enabled at 100MHz; (2) Output power - 15mA per LVDS pair, 25mA per LVPECL pair, 20mA per HCSL pair; (3) Total power - typically 150-200mW depending on output configuration and frequency; (4) Power-down mode - <10μA when disabled via I2C; (5) Individual output enable/disable - save power by disabling unused outputs. For power-sensitive applications, use LVDS outputs (lower power than LVPECL), disable unused outputs, and consider the 1.8V supply option (reduces power by ~30%). Thermal design should accommodate up to 250mW worst-case power dissipation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Typical 150-200mW; use 1.8V supply and disable unused outputs to minimize power.

products.name: 1:4 LVDS Clock Buffer
products.shortDescription: High-performance 1:4 LVDS clock buffer with additive jitter <50fs, supporting frequencies up to 2GHz for clock distribution applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The AU5411 is a high-performance 1:4 LVDS clock buffer designed for distributing low-jitter clock signals to multiple loads. It features ultra-low additive jitter of less than 50 femtoseconds, preserving the quality of the input clock signal.

Supporting input and output frequencies up to 2GHz, the AU5411 is suitable for high-speed networking, data center, and communication applications. The device accepts differential LVDS, LVPECL, or CML inputs and provides four identical LVDS output pairs.
The AU5411 operates from a 2.5V or 3.3V supply and is available in a compact 3x3mm QFN package. It features a synchronous output enable control and is pin-compatible with industry-standard buffers.
applications: Server and data center clock trees

faeReview.author: David Liu
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - High-Speed Design
faeReview.content: The AU5411 is an excellent choice for clock distribution when you need to fan out a precision clock to multiple loads. In my experience, the <50fs additive jitter is among the best in its class - I've measured actual performance around 30-40fs in well-designed systems. This means you can cascade multiple buffers without significant jitter accumulation. The flexible input stage is a nice feature - it accepts LVDS, LVPECL, or CML without external components, simplifying BOM. The pin compatibility with popular industry buffers makes it an easy drop-in replacement with cost savings. One practical tip: the output enable is synchronous, so you won't get runt pulses when enabling/disabling outputs - important for systems that need dynamic clock gating.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low additive jitter clock buffer ideal for distributing precision clocks to multiple loads

alternativeParts.reason: More outputs for complex distribution, higher jitter

companionParts.description: Clock generator to provide precision input clock

faqs.question: What is additive jitter and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Additive jitter is the additional jitter introduced by a clock buffer or distribution device beyond the jitter present in the input clock. It's important because jitter accumulates through the clock chain - each buffer adds a small amount of jitter to the signal. The AU5411's <50fs additive jitter means that even if you cascade multiple buffers, the total jitter increase is minimal. For example, cascading two AU5411 devices would add approximately 70fs total jitter (RSS combination), which is still excellent for most high-speed applications. When selecting clock buffers, always check the additive jitter specification - devices with >100fs additive jitter can significantly degrade clock quality in multi-stage distribution networks.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose buffers with low additive jitter (<50fs) for multi-stage clock distribution.
